# Death penalty sought for SC man accused in 'largest single murder' in Spartanburg Co. history

Death penalty sought for SC man accused in 'largest single murder' in Spartanburg Co. history

ACTOR MATTHEW PERRY. BUT FIRST, WE’RE COMING ON THE AIR WITH A LOT OF BREAKING NEWS. AT NOON, WE START OUT OF SPARTANBURG COUNTY, WHERE WE HAVE JUST LEARNED THE STATE WILL SEEK THE DEATH PENALTY FOR A MAN ACCUSED OF KILLING FIVE PEOPLE IN SPARTANBURG COUNTY IN OCTOBER OF 2022. JAMES DRAYTON IS CHARGED WITH FIVE COUNTS OF MURDER. HE WAS ARRESTED IN BURKE COUNTY, GEORGIA, AFTER DEPUTIES SAY HE STOLE ONE OF THE VICTIM’S CARS. AUTHORITIES SAY HE LIVED AT THE HOUSE ON BOBO DRIVE, WHERE FIVE PEOPLE WERE GUNNED DOWN AND KILLED. OFFICIALS TELL US AT THE TIME OF THE MURDER, THE HOME WAS A SAFE HAVEN FOR DRUG ADDICTS TO COME AND DO DRUGS. SPARTANBURG COUNTY SHERIFF CHUCK WRIGHT ALSO TELLS US HE GAVE A FULL CONFESSION TO SPARTANBURG COUNTY DEPUTIES AT THE TIME. A TRIA

Death penalty sought for SC man accused in 'largest single murder' in Spartanburg Co. history

Updated: 12:49 PM EDT Aug 15, 2024


[Editorial Standards ⓘ](https://www.wyff4.com/article/hearst-television-news-policy-statements/14471973)

[Stephanie Moore profile page](https://www.wyff4.com/news-team/42dcc6f1-07fb-42c6-a24d-d4bf38e6175e)

[Stephanie Moore](https://www.wyff4.com/news-team/42dcc6f1-07fb-42c6-a24d-d4bf38e6175e)[Stephanie Moore Email](mailto:smoore@hearst.com)

[Digital Media Manager](https://www.wyff4.com/news-team/42dcc6f1-07fb-42c6-a24d-d4bf38e6175e)

South Carolina is seeking the death penalty for a man accused in the largest single murder in Spartanburg County to date. James Douglas Drayton, 24, is accused of killing James Derek Baldwin, 49; Roman Christean Megael Rocha, 19; Thomas Ellis Anderson, 37; Adam Daniel Morley, 32; and Mark Allen Hewitt, 59. Authorities said four of the victims were found shot to death in separate rooms of a home on Bobo Drive in October 2022. One of the victims died at the hospital. (Video below is a news conference from Spartanburg County Sheriff Chuck Wright following Drayton's arrest) "He confessed to the crime," Wright said at the time of Drayton's arrest. "He basically said he'd been hearing voices. Not sure what that means for him, but he knew he'd been using meth and had been up for like four days. Hadn't slept in four days, probably not thinking."As for motive, Wright said drugs were involved. Wright said the home where the shootings happened was frequented by drug users, and all of the victims were believed to be using drugs. "These men and women didn't deserve what they got," Wright said. "They did not get justice at all. And just because we have someone in custody doesn't make things better for these families. It just means that they don't have to wonder."The death penalty notice signed on Aug. 12 said the trial date for Drayton will be set 30 days after the notice and after Drayton's lawyers are provided the evidence the state has in the case.


**SPARTANBURG COUNTY, S.C. —**

South Carolina is seeking the death penalty for a man accused in the [largest single murder in Spartanburg County](https://www.wyff4.com/article/suspect-quintuple-homicide-arrested-georgia-chase/41589771) to date.

James Douglas Drayton, 24, is accused of killing James Derek Baldwin, 49; Roman Christean Megael Rocha, 19; [Thomas Ellis Anderson](https://www.wyff4.com/article/loved-one-remembers-one-of-the-five-victims-found-dead-in-inman-home/41592196), 37; Adam Daniel Morley, 32; and Mark Allen Hewitt, 59.

Advertisement


Authorities said four of the victims were found shot to death in separate rooms of a home on Bobo Drive in October 2022. One of the victims died at the hospital.

_(Video below is a news conference from Spartanburg County Sheriff Chuck Wright following Drayton's arrest)_

![](https://hips.hearstapps.com/vidthumb/bba28b9f-b939-4985-8182-1e09d5692a32/bba28b9f-b939-4985-8182-1e09d5692a32_image.jpg?crop=1xw%3A1.0xh%3Bcenter%2Ctop&resize=810%3A*&quality=70)

"He confessed to the crime," Wright said at the time of Drayton's arrest. "He basically said he'd been hearing voices. Not sure what that means for him, but he knew he'd been using meth and had been up for like four days. Hadn't slept in four days, probably not thinking."

As for motive, Wright said drugs were involved.

Wright said the home where the shootings happened was frequented by drug users, and all of the victims were believed to be using drugs.

"These men and women didn't deserve what they got," Wright said. "They did not get justice at all. And just because we have someone in custody doesn't make things better for these families. It just means that they don't have to wonder."

The death penalty notice signed on Aug. 12 said the trial date for Drayton will be set 30 days after the notice and after Drayton's lawyers are provided the evidence the state has in the case.

# Fewer Americans will host cookouts this Independence Day. Here's why

## About 86% of Americans said they plan to celebrate the holiday this year.

Next Story

Fewer Americans will host cookouts this Independence Day. Here's why

About 86% of Americans said they plan to celebrate the holiday this year.

![](https://hips.hearstapps.com/vidthumb/ac9135e7-d0f9-4855-bfd9-3764ac07579d/thumb_1920x1080_00002_1750795942_6787.jpg?crop=1xw%3A1xh%3Bcenter%2Ctop&resize=810%3A*&quality=70)

✕

Some of the trends we're seeing this year with consumers and 4th of July is that the holiday is just as popular as it's ever been. In fact, Independence Day is the 2nd most popular holiday in the US following the winter holidays, and consumers are planning cookouts, they're going to fireworks displays, having picnics, and heading to parades. We are seeing this year that on average consumers are spending about what they spent last year, slightly more, $92 compared to $90 specifically on food and other items for their cookouts and barbecues. However, consumers total spending on these items is about. 8.9 billion compared to over $9 billion last year, 9.4 billion. The reason we see this difference between per person spending and total spending is that fewer people overall are planning these cookouts and barbecues, about 61% compared to 66% last year. We do believe this holiday remains as popular as ever, even if some of the individual ways consumers might be celebrating might be changing compared to previous years. We also know that the reason people celebrate is because they love the fun and the social aspects of the holiday, as well as the patriotic meaning that it has for many Americans.


![WYFF 4 logo](https://kubrick.htvapps.com/htv-prod-media.s3.amazonaws.com/htv_default_image/site_branding/wyff.png)

Updated: 2:14 PM EDT Jul 1, 2025

Fewer Americans will host cookouts this Independence Day. Here's why

About 86% of Americans said they plan to celebrate the holiday this year.

Updated: 2:14 PM EDT Jul 1, 2025


[Editorial Standards ⓘ](https://www.wyff4.com/article/hearst-television-news-policy-statements/14471973)

Damali Ramirez


Data Researcher/Curator


Nicki Camberg


Data Journalist


Fewer Americans will host a barbecue, cookout, picnic or other food-related event this Independence Day, according to an annual survey done by the National Retail Federation alongside Proper Insights & Analytics. Last year, 66% of Americans planned on hosting food-related events, but this year, that number dropped to 61%.Some of this shift may be due to the uncertainty around rising costs, causing consumers to pull back on pricier aspects of the holiday, said Katherine Cullen, vice president of industry and consumer insights at NRF.On average, consumers will spend $92.44 on food items for the holiday, slightly up from last year. The NRF’s annual survey polled about 8,000 adults on their Fourth of July spending plans and celebration expectations.The decrease in planned food-related events is reducing the total amount that celebrants will spend this year.In total, consumers will spend $8.9 billion on food items for July 4, a 5.3% decline from last year. It's now the second consecutive year total spending for the holiday took a dip. But even with food prices rising, the holiday still remains popular. About 86% of Americans said they plan to celebrate the holiday this year, making it the second most popular holiday, behind the winter holidays, said Cullen.


**WASHINGTON —**

Fewer Americans will host a barbecue, cookout, picnic or other food-related event this Independence Day, according to an annual survey done by the [National Retail Federation alongside Proper Insights & Analytics](https://nrf.com/research-insights/holiday-data-and-trends/independence-day/independence-day-data-center).

Last year, 66% of Americans planned on hosting food-related events, but this year, that number dropped to 61%.

Advertisement


Some of this shift may be due to the uncertainty around rising costs, causing consumers to pull back on pricier aspects of the holiday, said Katherine Cullen, vice president of industry and consumer insights at NRF.

On average, consumers will spend $92.44 on food items for the holiday, slightly up from last year.

[iframe](https://datawrapper.dwcdn.net/C192G/7/)

The NRF’s annual survey polled about 8,000 adults on their Fourth of July spending plans and celebration expectations.

The decrease in planned food-related events is reducing the total amount that celebrants will spend this year.

In total, consumers will spend $8.9 billion on food items for July 4, a 5.3% decline from last year. It's now the second consecutive year total spending for the holiday took a dip.

[iframe](https://datawrapper.dwcdn.net/pePtJ/7/)

But even with food prices rising, the holiday still remains popular.

About 86% of Americans said they plan to celebrate the holiday this year, making it the second most popular holiday, behind the winter holidays, said Cullen.
